steps.name: AS-REQ
steps.message: AS-REQ: identity + pre-auth timestamp (encrypted with password-derived key)
steps.description: The client requests a Ticket Granting Ticket, proving it knows the password by encrypting a timestamp (pre-authentication).
steps.security_note: If pre-auth is disabled for an account, the KDC returns encrypted material to anyone - enabling AS-REP Roasting.

steps.name: AS-REP
steps.message: AS-REP: TGT (encrypted with krbtgt key) + session key
steps.description: The KDC issues a TGT encrypted with the krbtgt account's key, plus a session key for future requests.
steps.security_note: The TGT is encrypted with the krbtgt hash. If that hash is stolen, an attacker forges TGTs for anyone - a Golden Ticket.

steps.name: TGS-REQ
steps.message: TGS-REQ: present TGT, request service ticket for an SPN
steps.description: The client presents its TGT and asks for a service ticket for a specific Service Principal Name.
steps.security_note: Any authenticated user can request a service ticket for any SPN - the basis of Kerberoasting.

steps.name: TGS-REP
steps.message: TGS-REP: service ticket (encrypted with the service account key)
steps.description: The KDC returns a service ticket encrypted with the target service account's key.
steps.security_note: Because the ticket is encrypted with the service account's hash, it can be cracked offline to recover that account's password.

steps.name: AP-REQ
steps.message: AP-REQ: present service ticket to the service
steps.description: The client presents the service ticket to the service to gain access.
steps.security_note: A service trusts any ticket it can decrypt. With the service hash, an attacker forges a Silver Ticket that never touches the KDC.

steps.name: AP-REP
steps.message: AP-REP: access granted (optional mutual authentication)
steps.description: The service validates the ticket and grants access, optionally proving its own identity to the client.
steps.security_note: There is no per-request check with the KDC, so forged or replayed tickets (Pass-the-Ticket) are accepted.

attacks.name: Kerberoasting
attacks.note: Request service tickets for SPNs and crack them offline to recover service-account passwords.

attacks.name: AS-REP Roasting
attacks.note: Target accounts with pre-auth disabled and crack the returned AS-REP offline.

attacks.name: Golden Ticket
attacks.note: Forge TGTs using a stolen krbtgt hash - full domain persistence.

attacks.name: Silver Ticket
attacks.note: Forge service tickets using a stolen service-account hash, bypassing the KDC.

steps.name: NEGOTIATE
steps.message: NTLM_NEGOTIATE: advertise capabilities
steps.description: The client requests NTLM authentication and advertises the features it supports.
steps.security_note: NTLM has no server authentication at this stage, which enables relay attacks in the middle.

steps.name: CHALLENGE
steps.message: NTLM_CHALLENGE: random 8-byte nonce
steps.description: The server sends a random challenge (nonce) for the client to sign.
steps.security_note: A relaying attacker can forward this challenge to a different target server the victim has access to.

steps.name: AUTHENTICATE
steps.message: NTLM_AUTHENTICATE: response = challenge encrypted with the NT hash
steps.description: The client returns a response computed from the challenge and its NT hash - the password itself is never sent.
steps.security_note: Since only the NT hash is needed, a stolen hash authenticates without the plaintext password - Pass-the-Hash.

steps.name: VALIDATE
steps.message: Netlogon: forward response to the DC for validation
steps.description: For domain accounts, the server forwards the response to a domain controller to verify it.
steps.security_note: If the response is relayed to a service that lacks SMB signing, the attacker authenticates as the victim.

steps.name: RESULT
steps.message: Validation result: success -> access granted
steps.description: The DC confirms the response and the server grants access to the client.
steps.security_note: Captured NTLMv2 responses can be cracked offline to recover the password.

attacks.name: Pass-the-Hash
attacks.note: Authenticate using a stolen NT hash without ever knowing the password.

attacks.name: NTLM Relay
attacks.note: Relay a victim's authentication to another service that lacks signing.

attacks.name: Hash cracking
attacks.note: Crack captured NTLMv2 responses offline to recover credentials.

steps.name: Credentials
steps.message: User supplies username + password
steps.description: A PAM-aware application (login, sshd, sudo) collects the user's credentials.
steps.security_note: The application delegates all policy to PAM, so a malicious application or PAM config controls the outcome.

steps.name: pam_authenticate
steps.message: Application calls pam_authenticate() through the PAM API
steps.description: The application hands the credentials to the PAM stack defined in /etc/pam.d.
steps.security_note: A rogue PAM module inserted into the stack can log passwords or grant access with a magic password.

steps.name: auth check
steps.message: pam_unix compares the hash against /etc/shadow
steps.description: The auth module (typically pam_unix) hashes the input and compares it to the stored hash.
steps.security_note: Read access to /etc/shadow lets an attacker crack hashes offline (John/Hashcat).

steps.name: account/session
steps.message: account + session modules apply authorization, limits, logging
steps.description: PAM enforces account validity (expiry, allowed times) and sets up the session.
steps.security_note: Weak pam.d configuration (e.g. nullok, or pam_permit) can silently allow blank or any password.

steps.name: Session
steps.message: Success -> shell / session opened
steps.description: PAM returns success and the application starts the user's session.
steps.security_note: Session modules are also a persistence point (e.g. a module that spawns a backdoor at login).

attacks.name: Malicious PAM module
attacks.note: A backdoored PAM module logs credentials or accepts a hard-coded password.

attacks.name: /etc/shadow theft
attacks.note: Steal the shadow file and crack password hashes offline.

steps.name: Version + KEX init
steps.message: TCP connect, protocol version exchange, algorithm negotiation
steps.description: Client and server agree on protocol version and the key-exchange and cipher algorithms.
steps.security_note: Weak or legacy algorithms should be disabled; scanning for open 22/tcp is a common recon step.

steps.name: Key exchange
steps.message: Diffie-Hellman KEX; server presents host key
steps.description: A shared session key is derived, and the server proves its identity with its host key (checked against known_hosts).
steps.security_note: If a user ignores a changed host key, a man-in-the-middle can intercept the session.

steps.name: Client auth
steps.message: publickey: sign the session with the private key (or send password)
steps.description: The client authenticates, typically by signing with its private key to prove possession.
steps.security_note: Stolen private keys grant access silently; password auth is exposed to brute force.

steps.name: Authorize
steps.message: Server checks ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (or password)
steps.description: The server verifies the key against authorized_keys, or validates the password.
steps.security_note: Adding a key to authorized_keys is a stealthy, common persistence technique.

steps.name: Session
steps.message: Encrypted channel established -> shell / tunnel
steps.description: An encrypted session opens; SSH can also forward ports and tunnel other traffic.
steps.security_note: SSH tunnels are used to pivot and exfiltrate over an allowed port (22).

attacks.name: authorized_keys persistence
attacks.note: Add an attacker public key to authorized_keys for silent, durable access.

attacks.name: Private key theft
attacks.note: Steal unprotected private keys to authenticate as the victim.

attacks.name: SSH brute force
attacks.note: Guess passwords against exposed SSH; mitigate with keys-only + fail2ban.
